text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 12]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   There are no explicit mathematical equations presented on this page.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   There are no graphs and charts on this page.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   There are no tables present on this page.

4.  **Diagrams and Schematics:**

    *   **Combustor Schematics (Multiple Views):** Several schematic diagrams illustrating various aspects of gas turbine combustor designs are present. These focus primarily on fuel and air introduction methods and liner geometry. Specific details include:

        *   A plenum system feeding fuel injection points (40 at each location) into a main combustion zone.
        *   A pilot zone with pilot fuel nozzles (20 pressure atomizers).
        *   Deswirl vanes located upstream of the combustor liner.
        *   Main stage nozzles introducing fuel and air into the main combustion chamber.
        *   Dilution and primary orifices for airflow directed at the liner wall. These show 20 springs attached.
        *   Injector types: 20 pressure atomizers (Test 1), and 20 air-assist airblast atomizers (Test 2). These are used in either the main or pilot staged.
        *   Swirling jets for ID (inner diameter) wall cooling.

    *   **Photographs of Combustors:** Several photographs display different annular combustor designs. One photograph in particular shows liner 'hot spots', identified to be in the 1550-1600F (843-871 C) range. Caption states these designs comes from data for 13 out of 43 combustors in the design.

title: 2004_Mongia__Perspective_of_Combustion_Modeling_for_Gas_Turb
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 13]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    The page contains no explicit equations. The graphs imply relationships between predicted and measured values, but the equations defining the "hybrid modeling" are not shown.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    All graphs on this page are scatter plots comparing the predicted values of different combustion parameters against their measured values, for 10 different combustor liners. The diagonal line in each plot represents a perfect agreement between predicted and measured data.

    *   **Graph 1: Predicted Pattern Factor vs. Measured Pattern Factor.**

        *   Axes: x-axis: Measured Pattern Factor; y-axis: Predicted Pattern Factor.
        *   Trends: The data points are scattered around the diagonal. Some data points tend to fall slightly below the diagonal, meaning model tends to under-predict a bit over the range.
        *   Key Data Points: Data clustered around a value of Pattern Factor = 0.2. Points reach up to Pattern Factor = 0.6, which suggests good agreement within the range of 0 to 0.6.
        *   Conclusion: Indication of a good agreement.

    *   **Graph 2: Predicted Smoke Number vs. Measured Smoke Number.**

        *   Axes: x-axis: Measured Smoke Number; y-axis: Predicted Smoke Number.
        *   Trends: Most data falls into 1 range of 40 to 60. The model appears to under predict some measurements.
        *   Key Data Points: There is a cluster of data at a measured smoke number near 50 and a spread of data at a measured smoke number ~10.
        *   Conclusion: The model has an acceptable agreement across the range. Possibly a systematic offset.

    *   **Graph 3: Predicted Fuel/Air Ratio vs. Measured Fuel/Air Ratio (Lean Blowout).**

        *   Axes: x-axis: Measured Fuel/Air Ratio; y-axis: Predicted Fuel/Air Ratio.
        *   Trends: Linear relationship.
        *   Key Data Points: The data is concentrated near a fuel/air ratio of 0.01. An outlier lies at around 0.02.
        *   Conclusion: Good linear agreement is observed.

    *   **Graph 4: Predicted UHC (g/kg) vs. Measured UHC (g/kg).**

        *   Axes: x-axis: Measured UHC (g/kg); y-axis: Predicted UHC (g/kg). Units are grams of unburned hydrocarbon per kilogram of fuel.
        *   Trends: Fairly linear relationship. The model tends to over predict at a measured values of 0-20 g/kg range compared to those greater than this range.
        *   Key Data Points: Points clustered near zero, and at 30 - 50 g/kg.
        *   Conclusion: General agreement, with deviation at smaller range.

    *   **Graph 5: Predicted CO (g/kg) vs. Measured CO (g/kg).**

        *   Axes: x-axis: Measured CO (g/kg); y-axis: Predicted CO (g/kg).
        *   Trends: There is a trend of positive linear correlation and good agreement.
        *   Key Data Points: The main cluster exists at zero. The secondary, measured values are around 40-50.
        *   Conclusion: Good agreement.

    *   **Graph 6: Predicted NOx (g/kg) vs. Measured NOx (g/kg).**

        *   Axes: x-axis: Measured NOx (g/kg); y-axis: Predicted NOx (g/kg). Units are

text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 16]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**
    There are no explicit mathematical equations presented on this page. The text references "Comb. Tube calculations" but not any equations or specific formulas.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**
    There are no graphs or charts displayed on the page.

3.  **Tables:**
    There are no tables displayed on the page.

4.  **Diagrams and Schematics:**
    There are three diagrams/schematics displayed.

    *   **Diagram 1 (Top Left):** This diagram appears to be a cut-away rendering of a gas turbine combustor. Labeled components include: "Fuel Nozzle," "Combustor Case," "Diffuser," "Cowl," "Swirler Assembly," "Inner Liner," "Outer Liner," and "Dome." The airflow direction and mixing processes within the combustor can be inferred. The presence of the fuel nozzle and swirler assembly indicates a premixed or partially premixed combustion system.

    *   **Diagram 2 (Top Right):** This is an engineering drawing, likely a cross-sectional schematic, of the gas turbine combustor. The finer details visible in this drawing suggest its purpose is for manufacturing and assembly, showing exact dimensions and construction of the combustor.

    *   **Diagram 3 (Bottom):** This is a photograph or rendered image of the combustor's swirler assembly and entrance to the combustor. The swirler is the set of vanes arranged to create a vortex to aid in flame stabilization, fuel-air mixing, and combustion. Together with the rest of the inlet configuration, the image helps understand the nature of the inlet flows.

The caption states that these are part of the "no-fudge factor" model validation process which seems to imply highly detailed models are being built with minimal tuning parameters allowed to better reflect reality. The seven types of combustor types cited are different annular combustors implying performance at variable swirler patterns.

text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 18]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   None visible on the page

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Bar Chart: Calculated Aeff within 3.5% error.**
        *   Axes:
            *   X-axis: Configuration (Config 10, Config 2, Config 6)
            *   Y-axis: % Error (from -4% to 4%)
        *   Data:  Shows percent error for Pilot, Cyclone, and Total effective areas for three configurations.
        *   Trends and Observations:
            *   In Config 10, the Pilot area has a noticeable negative error (under-predicted).
            *   In Config 2, both Pilot and Total have positive errors, indicating over-prediction of these areas.
            *   In Config 6, the error for both Pilot and Total is small.
            *   The Y axis indicates that they are visualizing the error in calculated effective area
            *   Maximum error for all configurations appears to remain within the +/- 3.5% bound.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   **Table 1: Comparison of Measured and Predicted Effective Areas**

        *   Columns: Measured Ae (in²), Predicted Ae (in²), % Error
        *   Data: Shows a series of measured and predicted effective areas with corresponding percentage error between them.  Example data points are: (0.321, 0.322, 0.19%), (1.700, 1.710, 0.59%).
        *   Overall: The data suggests generally good agreement between measured and predicted effective areas, with errors relatively small. The data suggests that a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulation is effective at predicting the effective area
    *   **Table 2: Nominal vs Calculated Areas**

        *   Columns: Ae (in²) Nominal, Ae (in²) Calculation, % Error, and rows of Primary, Secondary, and Total.
        *   Data: It is show difference in the effective area between Nominal (expected in the design) and calculated areas. Primary: (0.132, 0.119, 9.6%), Secondary (0.23, 0.236, 2.77%), total (0.362, 0.355, 1.74%)
   *   **Table 3: Area breakdown of Model Comparison**
        *   Columns: Area, Predicted, Measured, Delta (%) where Area consists of Pilot, Main I, and Main II
        *   Data: Provides comparison between predicted Vs Measured areas. Pilot (0.194, 0.2, 3%), Main I (0.962, 1.0, 4%), Main II(1.107, 1.14, 3%)

4.  **Diagrams/Schematics:**

    *   **CFD Visualization:** The page contains three 3D visualizations of a flow solver simulation showing the fluid flow through some type of mixer.
        *   *Mixer Geometry:* A structure containing vanes or inlets is illustrated to be driving flows inside of a combustor geometry.
        *   *Flow Visualization:* Streamlines with apparent color-coding based on velocity or a similar flow property are illustrating the complex fluid flow behavior downstream of the vanes or inlets.
    *   **Mesh Visualization:** A 3D visualization on a surface representing a mesh structure of a combustion system.
     *   *Mesh type:* Triangular surface elements
     *   *Purpose:* to show the mesh discretization that is necessary for CFD.

title: 2004_Mongia__Perspective_of_Combustion_Modeling_for_Gas_Turb
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 19]
**1. Mathematical Equations:**

There are no explicit equations written as text in the provided figure. However, the figure implicitly relates to models for turbulence, specifically the following turbulence models:

*   **k-ε (standard k-epsilon):** A two-equation turbulence model that solves for turbulent kinetic energy (k) and the rate of dissipation of turbulent kinetic energy (ε).
*   **Realizable k-ε:** A modification of the standard k-ε model to satisfy certain realizability constraints on the Reynolds stresses (not written explicitly).
*   **Renormalized k-ε (RNG k-ε):**  Another variant of the k-ε model derived using Renormalization Group (RNG) theory.
*   **k-ω:** A two-equation turbulence model solving for turbulent kinetic energy (k) and the specific rate of dissipation (ω).
*   **RSM (Reynolds Stress Model):** A more complex turbulence model that solves transport equations for each of the Reynolds stresses.

**2. Graphs and Charts:**

The figure contains several graphs categorized into left-hand and right-hand plots.

*   **Left-Hand Plots (a-d):** These show radial profiles of different quantities at a specific axial location, perhaps just inside the swirl cup.
    *   **(a) u_x (m/s) vs. r/D:** Axial velocity (u_x) is plotted against normalized radial distance (r/D), where 'D' is presumably a characteristic diameter of the swirl cup. Two curves exist, with the solid line labeled "TTV" and dotted line labeled current practice 2-D calculations. This plot shows a W-shaped profile, indicating a recirculation zone near the center (r/D = 0) and accelerating flow outwards. It is symmetric.
    *   **(b) u_r (m/s) vs. r/D:** Radial velocity (u_r) plotted against normalized radial distance (r/D). The plot likely shows two curves, again "TTV" and current practice. Radial velocity is antisymmetric around r/D=0.
    *   **(c) u_t (m/s) vs. r/D:** Tangential or Swirl velocity (u_t) plotted against normalized radial distance (r/D). The plot shows an antisymmetric swirl profile, possibly a Rankine vortex type profile. The solid and dotted line are labeled as before.
    *   **(d) k (m²/s²) vs. r/D:** Turbulent kinetic energy (k) is plotted against normalized radial distance (r/D). It shows a symmetric profile with peaks on either side. Two curves are reported of the similar nature as other plots.

*   **Right-Hand Plots:** These plots show axial velocity (m/s) profiles at different radial locations (Y, mm). The axial location X is fixed at 3 mm per the caption.
    *   Each plot has Y,mm on the y-axis spanning from 0-35mm. The X axes represent the axial velocities in m/s.
    *   Each plot includes data from LDV (Laser Doppler Velocimetry) experiments shown by the blue squares
    *   The predicted data is labeled "tetra"(possibly a mesh spacing), "walls" and "dldu(possibly a mesh spacing).

**3. Tables:**

There are no tables in the provided figure.

**4. Diagrams and Schematics:**

*   **Swirl Cup Schematic:** A 2D axisymmetric diagram of a swirl cup is present at the bottom left. The axial and radial flow directions are indicated. The streamlines are depicted as light blue lines. The recirculation zone is evident. Different turbulence models (RNG k-ω, RKE,

title: 2004_Mongia__Perspective_of_Combustion_Modeling_for_Gas_Turb
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 20]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**
    *   There are no explicit equations on the page. The models being compared (k-epsilon, Realizable k-epsilon, Re-Normalization k-epsilon, k-omega, and Reynolds Stress Model) are not defined by explicit equations on this page, but refer to specific turbulence models used in CFD.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**
    *   There are five sets of plots, labeled (a) through (e), each corresponding to a different turbulence model: Standard k-epsilon, Realizable k-epsilon, Re-Normalization k-epsilon, k-omega, and Reynolds Stress Model. Each set contains two subplots.
        *   *Subplot 1: Axial Velocity Contours:* These show a 2D contour plot of axial velocity in a swirl cup. The x-axis is the axial distance, labeled "X", ranging from 0 to ~160 (units are not specified). The y axis is not explicitly labeled but implicitely represents radius, with the plot showing a symmetric section of the swirl cup. Colors represent axial velocity magnitude: blue for low axial velocity, increasing towards green and red as velocity increases.
        *   *Subplot 2: Axial Velocity Profiles:* This shows the axial velocity profiles at different axial locations. The x-axis is the axial distance, labeled "X", ranging from 0 to ~160. This shows the data both as contours (as above) and as symbols from LDV (laser doppler velocimetry) measurements.

    *   Trends and Key Data Points:  The figures are comparing the simulation of axial velocity done using different methods versus experimental data. The LDV points are experimental, and the lines result from simulations.
    *   Conclusions: The plots allow for a qualitative comparison of the different turbulence models' ability to predict the axial velocity field in the swirl cup. The description says that the Standard k-epsilon model is "not as bad as thought". This implies the models are being compared to identify those most suitable for swirl cup calculations.

3.  **Tables:**
    *   There are no tables presented on the page.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**
    *   The contour plots can be seen as representing a schematic of the velocity field through the swirl cup. The simulations all involve similar geometry because they simulate the same domain, but the results differ qualitatively.

    *   **(a) Swirl cup, dome & part of nozzle:** A 3D rendered section of a gas turbine combustor swirl cup, dome, and nozzle assembly. The geometry is discretized into a computational mesh, likely for CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics) simulations. The mesh resolution varies, with finer mesh density in critical areas. The color scheme likely represents different material properties or zones within the component.
    *   **(b) Part of liner and dilution holes**: A 3D rendered section of the combustor liner showing dilution holes. Similar to (a), a computational mesh is overlaid onto the geometry. The liner likely experiences high temperatures, indicated by the use of red marking
    *   **(c) Diffusion system including swirl cup - 2.2 M nodes**: A 3D rendered view of a diffusion system including the swirl cup. The entire system is discretized into a computational mesh with approximately 2.2 million nodes.
    *   **(d) Combustor w/ one million nodes**: A 3D rendered view showing the meshing results for an entire combustor model consisting with approximately one million nodes.

The figure shows the grid generation technology employed to discretize the combustion systems to resolve finer details for accurate prediction of aero-thermal field. The text indicates that grid generation can be time-consuming.

title: 2004_Mongia__Perspective_of_Combustion_Modeling_for_Gas_Turb
text:

There are no explicit mathematical equations presented on this page.

**2. Graphs and Charts:**

*   **Title:** Whole Combustion System CCD Analysis - Non-Reacting Velocity Vector Plots
    *   **Description:** Several plots demonstrating velocity vector fields within a combustion system, likely during a non-reacting (cold flow) simulation. The figures show fluid flow patterns with vectors indicating direction and magnitude (indicated by vector size and color scale). Specific regions of interest are circled.
    *   **Axes:** No conventional x,y axes are present. However, the plots are 2D representations of flow fields within a 3D geometry. The colors shown on the legend indicate a range of velocities from 3.25e-02 to 2.44e+02 (units not explicitly stated in the provided section, but assumed to be consistent e.g. m/s depending on the simulation).
    *   **Trends and Observations:** The plots shows areas of recirculation, stagnation and directional flow through the combustion chamber. The velocity seems to be fairly low (blue-green) in the diffuser section and increases through the combustor (yellow-red).

**3. Tables:**

    | Prediffuser exit CP   | 0.505| 0.604 | 0.494 | 0.516 |

    | Mean Square Root Error (%) |  |13.30%|4.26%| 2.43% |12.76% |4.20%| 1.84%|

*   **Interpretation:** The table compares the predicted pressure recovery (CP in the table is likely "Coefficient of Pressure") with measured data at different locations within the diffuser, using different CFD settings. The "Center-peak Profile" and "Inner-peak Profile" likely refer to

title: 2004_Mongia__Perspective_of_Combustion_Modeling_for_Gas_Turb
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 24]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**
    *   K = dP / q_ann, where dP is the pressure drop and q_ann is the dynamic pressure.

2.  **Graphs:**

    *   Two contour plots are presented which show the calculated flow fields through a dilution hole.
    * X and Y axes show the flow geometry, pressure/velocity etc with units in meters.
    *   The left plot shows the discharge coefficient Cd for a pressure ratio parameter of K=10. The right plot shows the same for K=100.
    *   The color contours likely represent velocity, pressure, or a related quantity. The color bar for each plot indicates the value range (e.g. 0 to ~54 for K=10 graph).
    *   The trends show high-velocity regions near the orifice of the dilution hole.
    *   Key data points would involve extracting quantitative information from the contour plots correlated with the subsequent table.
    *   Conclusions should involve a comparison of the spatial distribution of velocity/pressure for the two K values (10 and 100).

3.  **Table:**
    *   The table shows the relationship between the pressure ration parameter 'K', discharge coefficient calculated from CFD ("Cd_CFD"), discharge coefficients from experiment "Cd_Data", then their percentage error (% Error), and resulting Jet angle.
    *   The table lists four different K values (10, 20, 50, 100) and corresponding data.
    *   As K increases, Cd_CFD increase from 0.5624 to 0.612
    *   As K increases, Cd_Data increase from 0.598 to 0.610
    *   % error ranges from -5.95% to 0.33%.
    *   The Jet angle increases from 64.92 to 80.36.
    *   Conclusion: The data shows that the discharge coefficient is affected by the pressure ratio parameter K, with a general increasing trend with an increase in K. Also the percentage error is relatively low when comparing with experiment.

4.  **Diagrams/Schematics:**
    *   The contour plots depict a cross-section of a dilution hole geometry. The primary features include:
        *   The main flow path upstream of the dilution hole inlet.
        *   The dilution hole itself, which is seen as an orifice.
        *   The region downstream of the dilution hole.
        *   The geometry implies a jet of fluid is injected into the main flow from the dilution hole.
    *   The contour plots depict high-speed flow concentrated in the dilution hole jet as described above.
    *   The schematics show the influence of 'K' on the dilution jet.

title: 2004_Mongia__Perspective_of_Combustion_Modeling_for_Gas_Turb
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 25]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**
    There are no explicit equations presented on this page.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**
    The figure presents two types: contour plots and a line plot.

    *   **(a) Log(CO) contours for 1750 & 1900K flames:**  Two 2D contour plots of the log(CO) concentration in a combustion chamber. The left contour is at a flame temperature of 1750 K, and the right one is at 1900K. Different colors signify different log(CO) levels; red is high, green is intermediate, and blue is significantly lower. The plots indicate the spatial distribution of CO within the combustion geometry. We see more CO overall with the 1750K contour.

    *   **(b) Log(NO) contours for 1750 & 1900K flames:** Similar to the CO plots, there contours of log(NO) for 1750, and 1900K flames. NO is a major species in combustion chemistry. These show the spatial distribution of log(NO); again, the higher temperature leads to significantly more NO.

    *   **Line Plot - Emissions vs. Flame Temperature:**  This plot has two y-axes and a single x-axis.

        *   **X-axis:** Flame Temperature (Tflame) in Kelvin, ranging from approximately 1700 K to 1950 K.

        *   **Left Y-axis:** Concentration of CO, NOx, and UHC (Unburned Hydrocarbons) in ppm (parts per million) corrected to 15% O2. It spans 0 to 100 ppm.

        *   **Right Y-axis:**  Concentration of NOx with different units than the left. It spans from 0 to 25.

        *   **Trends:**
            *   **NOx:** Generally increases with increasing flame temperature which makes sense as the production rate depends exponentially on temperature.
            *   **CO:** Initially decreases slightly with increasing temperature towards 1725K, then appears to show an increase with temperature.
            *   **UHC:** Decreases sharply as flame temperature rises indicating more oxidation.
3.  **Tables:**
    There are no tables present on this page.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**
    The contour plots in (a) and (b) show the geometry of the combustion chamber or a 2-D slice thereof. There are lines outlining what appears to be the walls. The colored fields represent the distributions of the logarithm of CO and NO, respectively. The colors indicate the concentration levels. In the 1750K case, the flame appears to reach much further into the chamber, which suggests that UHC is not completely consumed in the 1750K case.

title: 2004_Mongia__Perspective_of_Combustion_Modeling_for_Gas_Turb
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 27]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:** There are no explicit mathematical equations written out on this page. The graphs show correlations between data, but no equations defining them are present.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**
    *   **Temperature Contour Plot:** A 2D contour plot illustrating temperature distribution (in Kelvin) within a cross-section of the combustor (presumably the swirl cup). The colour scale indicates the magnitude of the temperature, with hotter regions in red and orange, and cooler regions in blue. The x and y axes are spatial coordinates (likely in mm or cm). The highest temperature appears near in the swirl cup. There is a legend to the side that indicates corresponding temperatures with colors

    *   **Non-dimensional Height Plot:** A plot showing non-dimensional height (ranging from 0 to 1) on the y-axis versus an unspecified parameter on the x-axis. Experimental data (circles) is compared against Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) results (line). The CFD line has a more direct and linear rate than the scatter plot of data points.

    *   **CO/NOx emissions vs. T3 (Left Graph):** The left chart plots the emission concentrations of CO (ppm) on the left y-axis (0-500 range) and NOx (ppm) on the right y-axis (0-300 range) against the temperature T3 (in degrees Fahrenheit) on the x-axis (300-1100 range). The plot includes data points (CO_Cell46, CO_G35 and Nox Cell46, Nox,G35). Model predictions are included with trends. The model predicts very low NOx prior to 700, and very high Nox at temperatures over 700.

    *   **CO/NOx emissions vs. T3 (Right Graph):** The right chart shows CO (ppm) on the left y-axis (0-400 range), NOx (ppm) on the right y-axis (0-200 range), and T3 temperature on the x-axis (300-1100 range). There are two sets of data on each axis: Test and Pred(predicted). The predicted variables increase in a very similar manner to the plot in the left of the chart. NOx emission increases rapidly after a temperature T3 higher than 600.

3.  **Tables:** There are no tables on this page.

4.  **Diagrams/Schematics:** The temperature contour plot itself could be interpreted as a simplified schematic of the combustor's flame zone. Visually, the plot shows a complex temperature distribution with localized hot spots.

        *   **Column (a): Liquid surface formation.**

        *   **Column (b): Onset of Breakup.**
            *   These plots show the initiation of the liquid breakup process. The red regions indicate the locations where the liquid is starting to disintegrate or break apart. The "onset of breakup" curves are observed to evolve from a thin arc to a more dispersed area as the J value decreased.

        *   **Column (c): Volume Flux at Onset of Breakup.**
            *   These contour plots likely show the volume flux (or volumetric flow rate, per unit area) of the liquid at the location where breakup starts. The colors likely represent different levels of volume flux, with warmer colors probably indicating higher fluxes. As J decreases, the color intensity increases, indicating a lower volume flux.

        *   **Column (d): Volume Flux 0.65" downstream.**
            *   Similar to column (c), these are contour plots of volume flux. However, they are taken at a location 0.65 inches downstream from the point of breakup. The units are given as microns (μ), which suggests a relative volume flux measurement but is likely an error in notation. It likely represents a measurement resolution. The contour areas expand as momentum flux ratio decreases.

        *   Axes: The axes are not explicitly labeled, but the x-axis is presumably distance along a section of the injector and the y-axis from the surface into the combustion chamber.
        *   Baseline Grid (224061): This plot shows the liquid film (red) and the surrounding space (blue), with a relatively coarse grid. One can see that the shape of the liquid film is determined with a limited amount of resolution.
        *   1st Level Refinement (297645): The plot shows a finer grid resolution. The resolution of the profile of the liquid film is improved versus the baseline mesh. The red portion is the developed liquid film.
        *   2nd Level Refinement (542778): The plot shows an even finer grid. Again, the resolution of the profile of the liquid film is further improved.
        *   Conclusion: Demonstrates increased grid resolution resolving liquid combustion modelling.

    *   *Predicted Liquid Film:* These plots show a 2D cross section of the predicted liquid film developing from the fuel injector. The liquid film is shown in red. There are three plots, showing the same geometry modeled with different levels of mesh refinement.

    *   *Comprehensive Mixer Analysis:* A 3D plot showing streamlines along the injector. Streamlines are colored, likely to represent velocity or pressure. This visualizes the flow field after air atomization.

It includes the CAD design, solid modeling, grid generation, mesh refinement, liquid film prediction, and flow field visualizations. The mesh refinement plots illustrate the importance of grid resolution for accurate liquid film modeling.

text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 31]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**
    *   There are no immediately apparent mathematical equations in the traditional sense (e.g., relating variables with equal signs). The table data could implicitly represent relationships, but these are not expressed as equations.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**
    *   **Temperature Contours:** This is a color contour plot, most likely of the combustion chamber. Color represents temperature. Red signifies high temperature regions, and as the color shifts to cooler colors (yellow to blue) shows lower temperatures.  It is likely that the plot shows the turbulent flame structure in the combustion chamber.
    *   **Spray Characteristics Graph:** This is XY scatter plot with two data series. The Y-axis is not shown, but the CFD Line indicates a profile and likely represents the spray concentration. The X-axis is not shown, and is unclear what data this corresponds to.

3.  **Tables:**
    *   **Table 1: Analytically predicted spray characteristics**
        *   Rows: represent results for low-power operation conditions or different zones numbered 1 through 4.
        *   Columns:
            *   *D32*: Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD) of the spray (probably in microns). This is the diameter of a droplet having the same surface-area-to-volume ratio as the entire spray.
            *   *Min Dia*: Minimum diameter of the droplet (probably in microns).
            *   *Max Dia*: Maximum diameter of the droplet (probably in microns).
        *   Summary: The table shows the spray characteristics for potentially different low-power operation modes or different zones. The D32, minimum diameter, and maximum diameter change significantly between the numbered zones, indicating different fuel spray characteristics.

    *   **Table 2: Combustor Exit Emissions**
        *   Rows: Alternating rows represent "DATA" (experimental measurements) and "CFD" (Computational Fluid Dynamics simulations) for each case or condition (likely corresponding to different operating points or radial locations).
        *   Columns:
            *   *CO (ppm)*: Carbon Monoxide concentration at the combustor exit in parts per million.
            *   *NO (ppm)*: Nitric Oxide concentration at the combustor exit in parts per million.
            *   *HC (ppm)*: Unburned Hydrocarbon concentration at the combustor exit in parts per million.
        *   Summary: The table compares experimental data with CFD results for CO, NO, and HC emissions. In general, the CFD model provides good qualitative agreement with the experimental data, although there are discrepancies in magnitude. For CO, the CFD underpredicts compared to the data.  For NO, the CFD underpredicts at low concentration and overpredicts at higher concentration. For HC, is also underpredicts the experimental data.

4.  **Diagrams/Schematics:**
    *   **Spray Schematic:** A schematic depicting a spray nozzle injecting fuel into a combustion chamber. The diagram shows trajectories forming a conical spray pattern.
    *   Summary: The schematic shows fuel being injected into a combustion chamber and the lines indicate the trajectories of the fuel injection.

text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 33]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    There are no explicit mathematical equations presented on this page. The analysis focuses on computational fluid dynamics (CFD) results, experimental data, and comparisons.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Plot: CPU Time per Iteration vs. Iteration**
        *   X-axis: Iteration
        *   Y-axis: CPU Time per Iteration [ms]
        *   Trend: The plot shows the CPU time per iteration decreasing rapidly in the initial iterations (0-100), then levels off to a nearly constant value with some small fluctuations as the simulation progresses to 1000 iterations.
        *   Key Data Points: Initially high CPU time per iteration drops dramatically.
        *   Conclusion: The graph demonstrates the convergence of the CFD simulation in terms of computational cost per iteration. The computational cost stabilizes after an initial transient period.

    *   **Contour Plots:** No mathematical equations.
        *   **Axial Velocity Contours:** shows the axial velocity distribution within a gas turbine combustor
            *   Colors: Colors correspond to defined velocity values shown by a legend, from -1.02e+02 values in purple to 4.50e+02 values in red (arbitrary units). High velocity regions are indicated in red, and low velocity regions in blue.
            *   Trend: A high-velocity airflow moving axially is apparent, most notably in the combustor inlet region
        *   **Mean Temperature Contours:** shows the mean temperature distribution within a gas turbine combustor
            *   Colors: Temperatures are coded from purple representing low temperatures (3.00e+02) values to red representing high temperatures (2.00e+03) values.
            *   Trend: A high-temperature region is observed downstream of the flame zone.

3.  **Tables:**

    The table presents a comparison of experimental data and CFD results for two cases.

    | Case | Bulk Equilibrium Temperature (Based on Total F/A, °F) | Pilot Equilibrium Temperature (Based on F/A, °F) | NO (Corrected to 15% O2, ppm) Data | NO (Corrected to 15% O2, ppm) CFD | CO (Corrected to 15% O2, ppm) Data | CO (Corrected to 15% O2, ppm) CFD |
    | ---- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
    | 1    | 2700                                                 | 3100                                                 | 8.9                                | 2.7                                   | 70                                 | 21                                  |
    | 2    | 2900                                                 | 3100                                                 | 10.5                               | 4.1                                   | 5                                  | 9.1                                  |

    *   **Summary:** The table compares bulk and pilot temperatures, NOx and CO emissions (corrected to 15% O2) from experimental data and CFD simulations for two different cases.
    *   **Observations:** The NOx emissions computed by CFD are significantly lower than the measured values in both cases. CO emissions vary in their agreement, but the second case exhibits noticeable differences. The "Case 1" has reduced bulk temperature when contrasted with "Case 2".

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**

    There are no diagrams or schematics present on this page of the document.
